# Model choice, priors & identifiability

So far we have improved sampling by tidying up priors and reparameterising. This
module pushes the idea one step further and asks a deeper question: **what
happens when the model itself cannot, in principle, separate two parameters —
and how can a prior rescue it?**

This is one of the most important ideas in the whole course. A model that a
classical (maximum-likelihood) analysis would reject as *unidentifiable* can be
perfectly usable in a Bayesian analysis — *provided* you can bring honest prior
information to the parameters the data cannot pin down.

## A deliberately broken model

Consider a simple linear regression, but with **two intercepts** instead of one:

```
model {
  for (i in 1:N) {
    y[i] ~ dnorm(mu[i], sigma)
    mu[i] <- alpha + alpha2 + beta * x[i]
  }
  alpha  ~ dnorm(0, 5)
  alpha2 ~ dnorm(0, 5)
  beta   ~ dnorm(0, 5)
  sigma  ~ dunif(0, 100)
}
```

Look closely at the mean: `alpha` and `alpha2` *only ever appear added
together*. The data see the sum $\alpha + \alpha_2$ — never the two pieces on
their own. Any increase in `alpha` can be exactly cancelled by an equal decrease
in `alpha2` with **no change to the fit**. The likelihood is completely flat
along the ridge

$$\alpha + \alpha_2 = \text{constant}.$$

Statisticians call this a **non-identifiable** parameterisation.
The *sum* is identified; the two *components* are not.

## Your task — part 1: watch the chains fail

1. Load the default example dataset (the built-in `y`, `x` data) in FANGS.
2. Paste the two-intercept model above and run it with **4 chains**.
3. Open the **Trace** tab and watch `alpha` and `alpha2`.

You should see them **wander** — slow, drifting, snake-like traces that never
settle, and that look different in every chain. Now check the **Summary** tab:

- **R-hat** for `alpha` and `alpha2` will be large (well above 1.01) — the
  chains disagree about where these parameters live.
- **ESS** will be tiny — successive draws are highly correlated, so thousands of
  iterations buy you only a handful of effectively independent samples.
- But look at `beta`, `sigma`, and (if you add it) the derived sum
  `alpha + alpha2`: these are **well-behaved**. Only the un-identified pieces
  misbehave.

::: callout-tip
The data constrain a *line* through $(\alpha, \alpha_2)$ space, not a *point*.
The sampler is free to slide anywhere along that line, so it does.
:::

## Your task — part 2: see the bimodality

Open the **Posteriors** tab and look at the density for `alpha` (and `alpha2`).
Because each chain settles in a different stretch of the ridge and mixes badly,
the pooled density often looks **lumpy or multi-modal** — two or more bumps
where you expected one smooth hill. That bimodality is **not a real feature of
the data**; it is an artefact of a non-identified parameter being explored by
chains that cannot agree. Compare it with the density of `beta`, which is a
single clean peak. Recognising "fake" multi-modality like this is a key
diagnostic skill.

## Your task — part 3: let a prior do the work

Here is the Bayesian punchline. The data cannot separate `alpha` and `alpha2`,
but a **prior can**. Tighten the prior on `alpha2` step by step and refit each
time, watching R-hat, ESS, and the posterior shape:

| Step | Prior on `alpha2`      | Prior SD | What it says |
|------|------------------------|----------|--------------|
| 1    | `alpha2 ~ dnorm(0, 5)` | 5        | "alpha2 could be almost anything" |
| 2    | `alpha2 ~ dnorm(0, 1)` | 1        | "alpha2 is probably small" |
| 3    | `alpha2 ~ dnorm(0, 0.2)`| 0.2     | "alpha2 is very close to 0" |

(Remember: FANGS parameterises `dnorm` by **standard deviation**, so the second
argument *is* the prior SD — a smaller number means a tighter prior.)

As the prior on `alpha2` tightens, it pins `alpha2` near zero. That **breaks the
symmetry of the ridge**: with `alpha2` held close to 0, the data can finally
identify `alpha` (it becomes the ordinary intercept again). Watch what happens:

- The `alpha` trace **stops wandering** and settles.
- **R-hat falls toward 1.00** and **ESS climbs** by orders of magnitude.
- The lumpy, bimodal density collapses into a **single clean peak**.

You have not added any data. You have not changed what the data can tell you
about the *sum*. You have simply supplied **external information** about one
parameter — and that was enough to make the whole model sample cleanly.

## The big idea

> A model that looks hopeless to a classical, likelihood-only analysis — because
> the data alone cannot identify every parameter — can be **perfectly usable in
> a Bayesian analysis** when you have genuine prior information about the
> parameters the data leave undetermined.

This is a real strength of the Bayesian approach, but it comes with
responsibility:

- The result now **depends on the prior** you chose for `alpha2`. That is fine
  *if the prior reflects real knowledge*, and dishonest if you tightened it just
  to make the sampler behave. Be transparent about where the information came
  from.
- Always check **how much the prior moves things** (exactly the prior-sensitivity
  habit from earlier modules). For an identified parameter the prior barely
  matters; for a non-identified one it does **all** the work — so say so.

## Record your runs

Refit the two-intercept model under **at least the three priors on `alpha2`**
in the table above. For each run, record the prior SD, the worst R-hat (of
`alpha`/`alpha2`), the minimum ESS, and a quick note on the posterior shape
(*ridge / bimodal / single peak*). Two or more rows unlock the module — and give
you a first-hand record of a prior turning an unidentifiable model into a
well-behaved one.
